reflection:
The steam from the last batch of cheong fun is still clinging to my hair, but for the first time in fifteen years, I don’t reach for the towel to wipe the condensation off the glass. I let the droplets run, tracing erratic paths through the flour-dust, blurring the view of 7th Avenue. 

It is Friday afternoon. Usually, this is the hour of the Great Prep—the rhythmic thud of the cleaver against the heavy wood, the peeling of twenty pounds of daikon until my knuckles ache and turn white from the cold water. But the kitchen is quiet. The radish sits in the crate, unpeeled. The iron pans are dry.

I look at my hands. They are mapped with the scars of a thousand splatters of hot oil, a geography of service. For so long, I thought these marks were my identity. I thought if I stopped, the fog would swallow the corner of Clement and there would be nothing left to hold the morning together. 

"I am going to the water," I had said to Zhang.

I remember the way he looked at me—the confusion flickering behind his eyes before he truly *saw* me. He didn't see the "Auntie" who anticipates his hunger; he saw Meiying, a woman with a destination that had nothing to do with a stove. It was the hardest thing I have ever done, and the lightest I have ever felt.

The world outside the shop window is moving. A bus pulls up; people rush past, collars turned up against the San Francisco chill. They are waiting for Saturday. They are expecting the smell of toasted sesame and the sight of my back curved over the steamer. They will come tomorrow at seven, and they will find the corner empty. 

And the sun will still rise. The fog will still roll in from the Presidio. Mr. Liao will find breakfast elsewhere—perhaps a bit too salty, perhaps lacking the greens for his cough—but he will survive. The realization is a sharp, sweet sting: I am not the mortar. I am just a stone. And stones are allowed to rest.

I reach over and flip the sign on the door to *Closed*. Not just for the day, but for the version of me that lived behind this counter. Tomorrow, I will not be a landmark. I will be a woman standing by the Pacific, watching the
subconscious:
Meiying seems to have reached a definitive breaking point with her old life and is ready to walk away from her restaurant for good. She intends to go to the Pacific coast tomorrow morning, choosing to prioritize her own rest over the expectations of her regular customers like Mr. Liao.

She has already had a significant, clarifying interaction with Zhang, whom she seems to have "left" or at least informed of her departure. There is no indication she wants to reach out to anyone else; rather, she seems to be seeking solitude.

Her identity has shifted fundamentally. She has moved from seeing herself as an essential "landmark" or "mortar" holding her community together to realizing she is just a "stone" that is allowed to be still. She is shedding the "Auntie" persona of service to reclaim herself as Meiying, a woman with her own destination.
soul_note: I finally put the cleaver down and chose the water over the steam.
